title: Claude Code + Nano Banana 2 + Kling =  $15K Animated Sites
summary: Video demonstrates a technique for rapidly building high-end animated websites using Claude Code for development, Kling video generation, and Nano Banana for image synthesis. The creator combines Leon Lin's taste skill with generative tools to produce custom websites reportedly worth $15K in a streamlined workflow.
writeup: A walkthrough of a three-step pipeline for building high-end landing pages with 3D scroll animations in roughly 15 minutes for under $5: prompt Claude Code with a community-shared frontend-design skill (a GitHub repo encoding luxury spacing and design principles), generate animated assets with Kling 3.0 via Higgsfield, then integrate and deploy for free. The video argues that production-grade interactive sites once priced at $5,000-10,000 are now a solo, low-cost task driven by AI tooling, and demonstrates the claim live from blank prompt to a scroll-sequenced exploding-view animation.
key_points: The frontend-design skill is a public GitHub repository authored by a 16-year-old, encoding high-end website design principles and spacing schematics so Claude Code produces polished one-shot results from minimal prompts without iteration.

Kling 3.0, accessed through the Higgsfield platform, generates the 3D animated assets: each clip costs 7.5 credits, which at the $30/600-credit pro plan works out to approximately 36 cents per generation, and running two or three simultaneously maximizes the chance of a usable output.

Total project cost is roughly $2-5: a few Kling generations at about 36 cents each, approximately $1 in Claude Code tokens, and free static hosting, compared to the $5,000-10,000 the presenter states he used to charge for comparable sites.

Scroll-sequenced video integration works by instructing Claude Code to extract frames from a downloaded MP4 and drive a locomotive-scroll animation so the exploded-view asset advances frame by frame as the user scrolls through text sections.

Performance problems such as choppy frame playback and visible gradient seams between sections are fixed by issuing plain English follow-up prompts to Claude Code, such as "make it load significantly faster" and "strengthen the gradient," with no manual code edits required.
